title: Female employment rate, 1980 vs. 2024
subtitle: Share of the working-age population who are employed. Figures refer to [national estimates](#dod:ilo-national-estimates) and may not be fully comparable across countries.
caption:
Female employment rate, 1980 vs. 2024
Share of the working-age population who are employed. Figures refer to national estimates and may not be fully comparable across countries.

This scatter plot compares the share of the working-age population who are employed in 1980 against the same metric in 2024 for a selection of countries. The horizontal axis represents the female employment rate in 2024, while the vertical axis represents the rate in 1980, both ranging from 0% to 100%. Circles are sized by population, with a legend indicating scales for 50 million and 200 million people. The data points are color-coded by region: North America, South America, Africa, Europe, Asia, and Oceania. Employment is defined in line with the 13th ICLS and includes the production of goods for own use, such as subsistence farming.

Most labeled countries appear below the diagonal line where 1980 and 2024 values would be equal, showing that the 2024 rate is higher than the 1980 rate for these nations. The United States, a large circle in the North American group, sits at roughly 48% in 1980 and moves to approximately 58% in 2024. Brazil and the United Kingdom show similar upward shifts, with 1980 values near 40-45% rising to roughly 50-55% in 2024. European nations like Italy, Spain, and the Netherlands cluster in the middle of the chart, with 1980 rates between 25% and 40% and 2024 rates extending toward 50% to 60%.

At the lower end of the 1980 scale, Bangladesh and Pakistan show rates around 10% to 15%, with their 2024 positions moving rightward to the 30% to 40% range. In contrast, Malawi and Burkina Faso appear high on the vertical axis, with 1980 rates near 75% to 90%, while their 2024 positions are not explicitly labeled with high horizontal values in the same way. Thailand maintains a high position on both axes, hovering near 80%. Nigeria appears far to the right on the 2024 axis near 80%, with a 1980 value around 35%. The Bahamas sits at the extreme right, approaching 90% in 2024, with a 1980 value near 25%. The overall distribution shows a general shift toward higher employment shares in 2024 compared to 1980 across the visible data points.
